topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box landed on my cluttered desk with a soft, almost insulting thud. I stared at the arrangement—a garish mix of lilies and some aggressively cheerful sunflowers—and immediately felt a tightening in my chest. No note. Just flowers. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscles strain in my neck. I picked up the vase, the stems cool and slick under my fingers, and surveyed the display with a profound sense of annoyance. Who sends these unsolicited things? It felt like a performance, a gesture made without any consideration for the actual recipient. I tossed the box onto the corner of my filing cabinet, the sound sharp in the quiet office. I needed coffee, black, and strong enough to scour the memory of this unexpected intrusion.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the bouquet, I kept my expression carefully neutral, a mask of polite disinterest stretched across my features. The vibrant colors of the roses seemed to mock the drab beige of my living room. I took the package, the paper crinkling under my grip, and walked straight to the entryway, bypassing the kitchen entirely. Inside, the arrangement sat there, demanding attention it clearly wasn't earning. I set it down on the hall table with exaggerated care, my movements stiff. I paced three steps back and forth, my hands running through my hair, a low, frustrated sound escaping my throat. This was unnecessary. A pointless flourish. I decided I would deal with this later, after I’d managed to regain a semblance of calm.

[story 3]

I found the flowers sitting on the counter, a splash of unwelcome color against the otherwise sterile surface. My sigh was audible, a deep, weary expulsion of air that seemed to vibrate in the small space. I didn't touch them right away. Instead, I circled the counter slowly, my gaze sharp and dismissive. Every petal seemed too bright, every stem too perfect, and the lack of any accompanying message felt like a deliberate slight. I picked up a nearby glass of water, my knuckles whitening as I gripped it. I took a long, slow sip, forcing myself to breathe evenly, though the tension coiled tight in my shoulders, refusing to release. It was just… too much effort for something so utterly meaningless.
